sentences.original: The policeman on the beat moved up the avenue impressively.
sentences.simplified: The policeman walks along the avenue with confidence.

sentences.original:
The
impressiveness was habitual and not for show, for spectators were few.
sentences.simplified: The impressiveness happened often and was not for show. There were only a few spectators.

sentences.original:
The time was barely 10 o’clock at night, but chilly gusts of wind with
a taste of rain in them had well nigh de-peopled the streets.
sentences.simplified: The time was almost 10 o’clock at night. Chilly winds blew through the empty streets. The air smelled like rain and made it cold.

sentences.original:
Trying doors as he went, twirling his club with many intricate and
artful movements, turning now and then to cast his watchful eye adown
the pacific thoroughfare, the officer, with his stalwart form and
slight swagger, made a fine picture of a guardian of the peace.
sentences.simplified: The officer walks down the street, trying doors as he goes. He spins his club with skillful movements. He looks around the busy street. His strong body and confident walk make him look like a protector.

sentences.original:
The
vicinity was one that kept early hours.
sentences.simplified: The area woke up early every day.

sentences.original:
Now and then you might see the
lights of a cigar store or of an all-night lunch counter; but the
majority of the doors belonged to business places that had long since
been closed.
sentences.simplified: Sometimes you see the lights of a cigar store or a late-night lunch. Most of the doors belong to closed businesses.

sentences.original:
When about midway of a certain block the policeman suddenly slowed his
walk.
sentences.simplified: The policeman walks down a block. He suddenly slows his walk.

sentences.original:
In the doorway of a darkened hardware store a man leaned, with an
unlighted cigar in his mouth.
sentences.simplified: A man leaned in the doorway of a dark hardware store. He had an unlit cigar in his mouth.

sentences.original:
As the policeman walked up to him the man
spoke up quickly.
sentences.simplified: The policeman walked up to him. The man spoke quickly.

sentences.original: “It’s all right, officer,” he said, reassuringly.
sentences.simplified: "It's okay, officer," he said in a calm way.

sentences.original:
“I’m just waiting for
a friend.
sentences.simplified: "I wait for a friend."

sentences.original: It’s an appointment made twenty years ago.
sentences.simplified: It is an appointment from twenty years ago.

sentences.original:
Sounds a little
funny to you, doesn’t it?
sentences.simplified: "It sounds a bit funny to you, right?"

sentences.original:
Well, I’ll explain if you’d like to make
certain it’s all straight.
sentences.simplified: I can explain if you want to be sure.

sentences.original:
About that long ago there used to be a
restaurant where this store stands—‘Big Joe’ Brady’s restaurant.
sentences.simplified: A long time ago, there was a restaurant. It was called "Big Joe" Brady’s restaurant. This restaurant stood where the store is now.

sentences.original:
”

“Until five years ago,” said the policeman.
sentences.simplified: "Five years ago, the policeman said something."

sentences.original: “It was torn down then.
sentences.simplified: "It was taken down at that time."

sentences.original:
”

The man in the doorway struck a match and lit his cigar.
sentences.simplified: The man stands in the doorway. He strikes a match. He lights his cigar.

sentences.original:
The light
showed a pale, square-jawed face with keen eyes, and a little white
scar near his right eyebrow.
sentences.simplified: The light showed a pale face with a square jaw. His eyes are keen and sharp. There is a little white scar near his right eyebrow.

sentences.original:
His scarfpin was a large diamond, oddly
set.
sentences.simplified: His scarfpin was big. It had a diamond that looked strange.

sentences.original:
“Twenty years ago to-night,” said the man, “I dined here at ‘Big Joe’
Brady’s with Jimmy Wells, my best chum, and the finest chap in the
world.
sentences.simplified: "Twenty years ago tonight, I ate here with Jimmy Wells. He was my best friend and a great guy."

sentences.original:
He and I were raised here in New York, just like two brothers,
together.
sentences.simplified: He and I grow up here in New York. We are like two brothers. We spend time together every day.

sentences.original: I was eighteen and Jimmy was twenty.
sentences.simplified: I was eighteen. Jimmy was twenty.

sentences.original:
The next morning I was
to start for the West to make my fortune.
sentences.simplified: The next morning, I start my journey to the West. I want to find my fortune there.

sentences.original:
You couldn’t have dragged
Jimmy out of New York; he thought it was the only place on earth.
sentences.simplified: You could not pull Jimmy out of New York. He thinks it is the best place in the world.

sentences.original:
Well,
we agreed that night that we would meet here again exactly twenty years
from that date and time, no matter what our conditions might be or from
what distance we might have to come.
sentences.simplified: We agreed that night to meet again in twenty years. We said this no matter our conditions or how far we come.

sentences.original:
We figured that in twenty years
each of us ought to have our destiny worked out and our fortunes made,
whatever they were going to be.
sentences.simplified: We thought that in twenty years we will plan our lives. We also believe we will have money and success.

sentences.original:
”

“It sounds pretty interesting,” said the policeman.
sentences.simplified: "It sounds interesting," the policeman said.

sentences.original:
“Rather a long time
between meets, though, it seems to me.
sentences.simplified: "It seems to me that we wait a long time between meetings."

sentences.original:
Haven’t you heard from your
friend since you left?
sentences.simplified: "Did you not hear from your friend since you left?"

sentences.original:
”

“Well, yes, for a time we corresponded,” said the other.
sentences.simplified: "Yes, we wrote to each other for a time," said the other.

sentences.original:
“But after a
year or two we lost track of each other.
sentences.simplified: "But after one or two years, we stopped talking to each other."

sentences.original:
You see, the West is a pretty
big proposition, and I kept hustling around over it pretty lively.
sentences.simplified: The West is a big idea. I worked hard on it every day.

sentences.original:
But
I know Jimmy will meet me here if he’s alive, for he always was the
truest, stanchest old chap in the world.
sentences.simplified: I know Jimmy will meet me here if he is alive. He was always a true friend to me.

sentences.original: He’ll never forget.
sentences.simplified: He will always remember.

sentences.original:
I came a
thousand miles to stand in this door to-night, and it’s worth it if my
old partner turns up.
sentences.simplified: I traveled a long way to stand here tonight. It is worth it if my old partner arrives.

sentences.original:
”

The waiting man pulled out a handsome watch, the lids of it set with
small diamonds.
sentences.simplified: The waiting man took out a nice watch. It had small diamonds on its lid.

sentences.original: “Three minutes to ten,” he announced.
sentences.simplified: “It is three minutes to ten,” he said.

sentences.original:
“It was exactly ten o’clock when
we parted here at the restaurant door.
sentences.simplified: "It was ten o’clock when we said goodbye at the restaurant."

sentences.original:
”

“Did pretty well out West, didn’t you?
sentences.simplified: "You did well out West, didn't you?"

sentences.original: ” asked the policeman.
sentences.simplified: “asked the policeman.”

sentences.original: “You bet!
sentences.simplified: "You can be sure of that!"

sentences.original: I hope Jimmy has done half as well.
sentences.simplified: I hope Jimmy did as well as this.

sentences.original:
He was a kind of plodder,
though, good fellow as he was.
sentences.simplified: He was a slow worker. He was a good man too.

sentences.original:
I’ve had to compete with some of the
sharpest wits going to get my pile.
sentences.simplified: I had to compete with very smart people. I wanted to win my pile.

sentences.original: A man gets in a groove in New York.
sentences.simplified: A man finds his place in New York.

sentences.original: It takes the West to put a razor-edge on him.
sentences.simplified: It takes the West to make him sharp.

sentences.original:
”

The policeman twirled his club and took a step or two.
sentences.simplified: The policeman spun his club. He took one or two steps forward.

sentences.original: “I’ll be on my way.
sentences.simplified: "I am going now."

sentences.original: Hope your friend comes around all right.
sentences.simplified: I hope your friend comes back safely.

sentences.original:
Going to
call time on him sharp?
sentences.simplified: Are you going to call time on him soon?

sentences.original:
”

“I should say not!
sentences.simplified: "I really do not think so."

sentences.original: ” said the other.
sentences.simplified: "said the other."

sentences.original:
“I’ll give him half an hour at
least.
sentences.simplified: "I will give him at least thirty minutes."

sentences.original: If Jimmy is alive on earth he’ll be here by that time.
sentences.simplified: Jimmy is alive on earth. He will be here by that time.

sentences.original:
So long,
officer.
sentences.simplified: Goodbye, officer. I hope you have a nice day.

sentences.original:
”

“Good-night, sir,” said the policeman, passing on along his beat,
trying doors as he went.
sentences.simplified: “Good night, sir,” said the policeman. He walks along his beat. He tries doors as he goes.

sentences.original:
There was now a fine, cold drizzle falling, and the wind had risen from
its uncertain puffs into a steady blow.
sentences.simplified: A fine, cold rain falls now. The wind rises from soft puffs to a steady blow.

sentences.original:
The few foot passengers astir
in that quarter hurried dismally and silently along with coat collars
turned high and pocketed hands.
sentences.simplified: The few people walking in that area hurry silently. They pull up their coat collars and put their hands in their pockets.

sentences.original:
And in the door of the hardware store
the man who had come a thousand miles to fill an appointment, uncertain
almost to absurdity, with the friend of his youth, smoked his cigar and
waited.
sentences.simplified: A man stands at the door of a hardware store. He comes a long way to meet an old friend. He feels uncertain about this meeting. He smokes his cigar and waits quietly.

sentences.original:
About twenty minutes he waited, and then a tall man in a long overcoat,
with collar turned up to his ears, hurried across from the opposite
side of the street.
sentences.simplified: He waited for about twenty minutes. Then a tall man hurried over. The man wore a long coat with the collar turned up.

sentences.original: He went directly to the waiting man.
sentences.simplified: He went straight to the man who waited.

sentences.original: “Is that you, Bob?
sentences.simplified: "Is that really you, Bob?"

sentences.original: ” he asked, doubtfully.
sentences.simplified: He asked with doubt.

sentences.original: “Is that you, Jimmy Wells?
sentences.simplified: "Is that you, Jimmy?"

sentences.original: ” cried the man in the door.
sentences.simplified: The man cried from the door.

sentences.original: “Bless my heart!
sentences.simplified: "That is sweet!"

sentences.original:
” exclaimed the new arrival, grasping both the other’s
hands with his own.
sentences.simplified: The new person shouted. He took both of their hands.

sentences.original: “It’s Bob, sure as fate.
sentences.simplified: “It’s Bob, I am sure of it.”

sentences.original:
I was certain I’d find you
here if you were still in existence.
sentences.simplified: I was sure I would find you here. I thought you were still alive.

sentences.original: Well, well, well!
sentences.simplified: I am surprised and happy.

sentences.original:
—twenty years is
a long time.
sentences.simplified: Twenty years feels like a long time.

sentences.original:
The old restaurant’s gone, Bob; I wish it had lasted, so
we could have had another dinner there.
sentences.simplified: The old restaurant is gone. I wish it lasted longer. We could have dinner there again.

sentences.original:
How has the West treated you,
old man?
sentences.simplified: How does the West treat you, old man?

sentences.original:
”

“Bully; it has given me everything I asked it for.
sentences.simplified: "Bully has given me everything I wanted."

sentences.original:
You’ve changed lots,
Jimmy.
sentences.simplified: You change a lot, Jimmy.

sentences.original: I never thought you were so tall by two or three inches.
sentences.simplified: I never thought you were two or three inches taller.

sentences.original:
”

“Oh, I grew a bit after I was twenty.
sentences.simplified: "Oh, I grew a little after I turned twenty."

sentences.original:
”

“Doing well in New York, Jimmy?
sentences.simplified: "Are you doing well in New York, Jimmy?"

sentences.original:
”

“Moderately.
sentences.simplified: "Somewhat."

sentences.original: I have a position in one of the city departments.
sentences.simplified: I work in a city department.

sentences.original:
Come on,
Bob; we’ll go around to a place I know of, and have a good long talk
about old times.
sentences.simplified: Come on, Bob. We will go to a place I know. We can have a long talk about good times.

sentences.original:
”

The two men started up the street, arm in arm.
sentences.simplified: The two men walk up the street. They hold each other's arms.

sentences.original:
The man from the West,
his egotism enlarged by success, was beginning to outline the history
of his career.
sentences.simplified: The man from the West had success. His pride grew large. He started to tell the story of his career.

sentences.original:
The other, submerged in his overcoat, listened with
interest.
sentences.simplified: The other person wore his big coat. He listened with interest.

sentences.original: At the corner stood a drug store, brilliant with electric lights.
sentences.simplified: A drug store stood at the corner. It was bright with electric lights.

sentences.original:
When
they came into this glare each of them turned simultaneously to gaze
upon the other’s face.
sentences.simplified: When they entered the bright light, they turned to look at each other.

sentences.original: The man from the West stopped suddenly and released his arm.
sentences.simplified: The man from the West stopped suddenly. He let go of his arm.

sentences.original: “You’re not Jimmy Wells,” he snapped.
sentences.simplified: "You are not Jimmy Wells," he said sharply.

sentences.original:
“Twenty years is a long time, but
not long enough to change a man’s nose from a Roman to a pug.
sentences.simplified: Twenty years is a long time. It is not enough to change a man's nose. A Roman nose stays the same, not like a pug's.

sentences.original:
”

“It sometimes changes a good man into a bad one,” said the tall man.
sentences.simplified: "It sometimes changes a good man to a bad man," said the tall man.

sentences.original: “You’ve been under arrest for ten minutes, ‘Silky’ Bob.
sentences.simplified: "You are under arrest for ten minutes, 'Silky' Bob."

sentences.original:
Chicago thinks
you may have dropped over our way and wires us she wants to have a chat
with you.
sentences.simplified: Chicago thinks you came by and wants to talk with you.

sentences.original: Going quietly, are you?
sentences.simplified: Are you going quietly now?

sentences.original: That’s sensible.
sentences.simplified: That is a smart idea.

sentences.original:
Now, before we go on
to the station here’s a note I was asked to hand you.
sentences.simplified: Now, before we go to the station, I have a note for you.

sentences.original:
You may read it
here at the window.
sentences.simplified: You can read it here by the window.

sentences.original: It’s from Patrolman Wells.
sentences.simplified: It comes from Patrolman Wells.

sentences.original:
”

The man from the West unfolded the little piece of paper handed him.
sentences.simplified: The man from the West opened the small piece of paper. Someone gave it to him.

sentences.original:
His hand was steady when he began to read, but it trembled a little by
the time he had finished.
sentences.simplified: His hand is steady when he starts to read. It shakes a little when he finishes.

sentences.original: The note was rather short.
sentences.simplified: The note is very short.

sentences.original: _Bob: I was at the appointed place on time.
sentences.simplified: Bob was at the right place. He was there on time.

sentences.original:
When you struck the match
to light your cigar I saw it was the face of the man wanted in Chicago.
sentences.simplified: When you struck the match to light your cigar, I saw a man. He was the man wanted in Chicago.

sentences.original:
Somehow I couldn’t do it myself, so I went around and got a plain
clothes man to do the job.
sentences.simplified: I could not do it alone. I asked a man in plain clothes to help.

sentences.original: JIMMY.
sentences.simplified: Jimmy.

simplifiedText: The policeman walks along the avenue with confidence. The impressiveness happened often and was not for show. There were only a few spectators. The time was almost 10 o’clock at night. Chilly winds blew through the empty streets. The air smelled like rain and made it cold. The officer walks down the street, trying doors as he goes. He spins his club with skillful movements. He looks around the busy street. His strong body and confident walk make him look like a protector. The area woke up early every day. Sometimes you see the lights of a cigar store or a late-night lunch. Most of the doors belong to closed businesses. The policeman walks down a block. He suddenly slows his walk. A man leaned in the doorway of a dark hardware store. He had an unlit cigar in his mouth. The policeman walked up to him. The man spoke quickly. "It's okay, officer," he said in a calm way. "I wait for a friend." It is an appointment from twenty years ago. "It sounds a bit funny to you, right?" I can explain if you want to be sure. A long time ago, there was a restaurant. It was called "Big Joe" Brady’s restaurant. This restaurant stood where the store is now. "Five years ago, the policeman said something." "It was taken down at that time." The man stands in the doorway. He strikes a match. He lights his cigar. The light showed a pale face with a square jaw. His eyes are keen and sharp. There is a little white scar near his right eyebrow. His scarfpin was big. It had a diamond that looked strange. "Twenty years ago tonight, I ate here with Jimmy Wells. He was my best friend and a great guy." He and I grow up here in New York. We are like two brothers. We spend time together every day. I was eighteen. Jimmy was twenty. The next morning, I start my journey to the West. I want to find my fortune there. You could not pull Jimmy out of New York. He thinks it is the best place in the world. We agreed that night to meet again in twenty years. We said this no matter our conditions or how far we come. We thought that in twenty years we will plan our lives. We also believe we will have money and success. "It sounds interesting," the policeman said. "It seems to me that we wait a long time between meetings." "Did you not hear from your friend since you left?" "Yes, we wrote to each other for a time," said the other. "But after one or two years, we stopped talking to each other." The West is a big idea. I worked hard on it every day. I know Jimmy will meet me here if he is alive. He was always a true friend to me. He will always remember. I traveled a long way to stand here tonight. It is worth it if my old partner arrives. The waiting man took out a nice watch. It had small diamonds on its lid. “It is three minutes to ten,” he said. "It was ten o’clock when we said goodbye at the restaurant." "You did well out West, didn't you?" “asked the policeman.” "You can be sure of that!" I hope Jimmy did as well as this. He was a slow worker. He was a good man too. I had to compete with very smart people. I wanted to win my pile. A man finds his place in New York. It takes the West to make him sharp. The policeman spun his club. He took one or two steps forward. "I am going now." I hope your friend comes back safely. Are you going to call time on him soon? "I really do not think so." "said the other." "I will give him at least thirty minutes." Jimmy is alive on earth. He will be here by that time. Goodbye, officer. I hope you have a nice day. “Good night, sir,” said the policeman. He walks along his beat. He tries doors as he goes. A fine, cold rain falls now. The wind rises from soft puffs to a steady blow. The few people walking in that area hurry silently. They pull up their coat collars and put their hands in their pockets. A man stands at the door of a hardware store. He comes a long way to meet an old friend. He feels uncertain about this meeting. He smokes his cigar and waits quietly. He waited for about twenty minutes. Then a tall man hurried over. The man wore a long coat with the collar turned up. He went straight to the man who waited. "Is that really you, Bob?" He asked with doubt. "Is that you, Jimmy?" The man cried from the door. "That is sweet!" The new person shouted. He took both of their hands. “It’s Bob, I am sure of it.” I was sure I would find you here. I thought you were still alive. I am surprised and happy. Twenty years feels like a long time. The old restaurant is gone. I wish it lasted longer. We could have dinner there again. How does the West treat you, old man? "Bully has given me everything I wanted." You change a lot, Jimmy. I never thought you were two or three inches taller. "Oh, I grew a little after I turned twenty." "Are you doing well in New York, Jimmy?" "Somewhat." I work in a city department. Come on, Bob. We will go to a place I know. We can have a long talk about good times. The two men walk up the street. They hold each other's arms. The man from the West had success. His pride grew large. He started to tell the story of his career. The other person wore his big coat. He listened with interest. A drug store stood at the corner. It was bright with electric lights. When they entered the bright light, they turned to look at each other. The man from the West stopped suddenly. He let go of his arm. "You are not Jimmy Wells," he said sharply. Twenty years is a long time. It is not enough to change a man's nose. A Roman nose stays the same, not like a pug's. "It sometimes changes a good man to a bad man," said the tall man. "You are under arrest for ten minutes, 'Silky' Bob." Chicago thinks you came by and wants to talk with you. Are you going quietly now? That is a smart idea. Now, before we go to the station, I have a note for you. You can read it here by the window. It comes from Patrolman Wells. The man from the West opened the small piece of paper. Someone gave it to him. His hand is steady when he starts to read. It shakes a little when he finishes. The note is very short. Bob was at the right place. He was there on time. When you struck the match to light your cigar, I saw a man. He was the man wanted in Chicago. I could not do it alone. I asked a man in plain clothes to help. Jimmy.
